data.title: SA analyst rating changes: ORCL, NVDA, RDDT, PLCE, DG, POWL, CNDT
data.description:
Here are the latest Seeking Alpha analyst rating changes:

Upgrades:

Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA). Hold to buy by Johnny Zhang. "NVDA's robust growth momentum and strong balance sheet help the company navigate a potential recession scenario. Its supply constraints for AI chips provide a cushion against demand weakness during recession."

Oracle (NYSE:ORCL). Hold to buy by Juxtaposed Ideas. "With ORCL stock now trading (somewhat) nearer to historical trends and its peers, its high-growth investment thesis is more compelling as the management guides double-digits cloud growth opportunities."

Reddit (NYSE:RDDT). Hold to buy by Julian Lin. "Despite tariff uncertainties, RDDT's low ARPU compared to peers suggests sustained 20% top-line growth over the next decade."

Children's Place (NASDAQ:PLCE). Sell to hold by Jeffrey Fischer. "Given the new strategic direction and improved profitability, I upgrade PLCE to a Hold, though the tough competitive landscape for Children's Place remains."

Powell Industries (NASDAQ:POWL). Hold to buy by Juxtaposed Ideas. "Upgraded to a Buy due to dual return prospects from capital appreciation and dividend income, with POWL now trading well below its fair value estimates."

Downgrades: 

Dollar General (NYSE:DG). Hold to sell by Eleceed Capital. "I downgrade Dollar General from hold to sell due to poor same-store-sales growth and EBIT margin, worsened by deteriorating macro conditions and tariffs. DG, typically benefiting from economic downturns, faces intensified demand and margin pressure from tariffs and constrained consumer budgets."

Conduent (NASDAQ:CNDT). Buy to hold by Robert F. Abbott. "Despite positive signs, including institutional interest, I now rate CNDT a Hold due to uncertainty about market recognition and external economic factors."

data.title: OpenAI said to be working on building a social network: report
data.description:
OpenAI, the generative artificial intelligence startup backed by Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) and others, is working on building a social network, The Verge reported.

The project is still in the early stages, but an internal prototype includes a social feed that is focused on ChatGPT’s image generation, the news outlet added, citing sources familiar with the matter.

It's unclear if OpenAI would release the social network as a separate app or as part of ChatGPT, which is used by roughly 10% of the planet, Chief Executive Sam Altman said recently.

OpenAI did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

The news comes as the feud between Altman and Elon Musk, who previously co-founded OpenAI but is now suing the company (along with Microsoft and others), has heated up. In February, a Musk-led group made an all-cash offer for OpenAI to the tune of $97.4B, which Altman subsequently denied. Following the initial report of the offer, Altman appeared to respond and said, "[N]o thank you, but we will buy twitter [sic] for $9.74 billion if you want."

Twitter, now known as X, was recently acquired in an all-stock deal by Musk's OpenAI competitor, xAI, last month.

The Grok chatbot, which recently launched a standalone app, is integrated with X and uses posts to help train its AI models.

data.title: Bets against utility stocks slightly decline in March; AEP most shorted
data.description:
Bets against Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLU) marginally declined in March compared to the end of February, with American Electric Power (AEP) being the most shorted stock in the sector and Sempra (SRE) taking the spot for the least shorted.

Average short interest across S&P 500 utilities stocks was 2.29% of shares float at the end of March, slightly down from 2.31% at the end of February.

The S&P 500’s Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLU) was up 2.76% so far this year, outperforming the broader S&P500 market, which declined over 8% during the same period.

Stocks with the least and largest short positions

Average short interest as a percentage of floating shares

American Electric Power (AEP) had 24.6 million shares sold short as of March 31, or 4.62% of shares float.

CenterPoint Energy (CNP) was the second-most shorted stock with short interest of 4.14% followed by Evergy (EVRG) with 4.09%, AES (AES) with 3.98% and WEC Energy Group (WEC) with 3.85%.

Sempra (SRE) was the least shorted stock, with 7.73 million shares sold short or 1.22% of the shares float, followed by Xcel Energy (XEL) and Public Service Enterprise Group (PEG) with short interest of 1.48% and 1.49% respectively.

Industry analysis

Average short interest as a percentage of floating shares

Independent Power & Renewable Electricity Producers was the most shorted industry within the sector with 3.45% short interest as of March end, compared to 4.40% a month ago.

Gas Utilities stood at the second spot with 3.08% short interest as of the end of March, which decreased from 3.20% at the end of February.

Water Utilities stocks took the third spot with 2.36% short interest as of March end, compared to 2.62% at the end of February.

Bets against the Electric Utilities sector as a whole have increased to 2.17% at the end of March, compared to 2.01% a month ago.

data.title: Ironwood downgraded at Jefferies on apraglutide-related challenges
data.description:
• Ironwood Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:IRWD) has been downgraded to Hold from Buy at Jefferies after the company announced pressures related to its lead asset, apraglutide. 
  • The company announced Monday that it has decided to evaluate strategic alternatives after the FDA required an additional Phase 3 trial to secure approval for its apraglutide.
  • The downgrade by Jefferies reflects increased uncertainty around the drug’s development timeline and approval prospects, with analysts saying that while they previously viewed apraglutide’s clinical profile as a strength, the need for another pivotal study introduces a delay in potential approval, now expected no earlier than 2029, and adds regulatory and financial uncertainty. 
  • Jefferies has lowered its PT to $0.70 from $8 per share.

data.title: Chinese developer Xinyuan said to face involuntary bankruptcy as creditors demand payment
data.description:
Xinyuan Real Estate's (NYSE:XIN) creditors recently filed for involuntary Chapter 11 bankruptcy petition in New York to recover $65.8M in unpaid bonds, making it one of many Chinese builders grappling with financial turmoil in recent years, according to a Tuesday media report that cited a court filing.

Under U.S. bankruptcy law, creditors can request court oversight if a company misses debt payments, allowing the firm to either contest the petition or enter bankruptcy to reorganize.

The company could use its U.S.-based assets to help repay the bonds, the bondholders said in the bankruptcy filing, Bloomberg reported. 

XIN stock traded 14% lower in Tuesday midday trading in the U.S., changing hands at $2.24 per share.

Xinyuan (NYSE:XIN) missed an interest payment in 2022, followed by a failed $170M bond payment that came due in 2024, the filing also showed.

The petition was filed Monday evening at the U.S. Bankruptcy Court for the Southern District of New York.

data.title: It's too early to call “all-clear” amid continued bond volatility - MS
data.description:
It might be too soon to call “all-clear” while there is still bond volatility, according to Morgan Stanley’s CIO.

Lisa Shalett, Morgan Stanley Wealth Management’s chief investment officer, said markets have partially recalibrated after recent volatility, with oversold technicals driving a rebound in lagging sectors like financials (XLF).

“The market is behaving in a very rational manner in the ultra-short term,” she told CNBC during an interview, though she cautioned against declaring an “all clear” given persistent macroeconomic risks.

Earnings revisions remain a critical concern, with Morgan Stanley’s Mike Wilson cutting his 2025 S&P 500 (SP500) EPS forecast to $257 --  down from $271 -- amid slowing growth. Wilson also revised its 2026 EPS forecast down to $281 from $303.

Shalett emphasized that equities still trade at over 20x forward P/E ratios, urging investors to avoid “getting too far over your skis” despite selective bargains.

“Our base case is a soft landing, but barely,” she added, noting a 40% recession probability.

She expressed heightened concern over U.S. Treasury market stability, telling CNBC that bond vigilantes are scrutinizing fiscal deficits and global currency dynamics.

“We’re not at all out of the woods,” she said, highlighting risks from hedge fund basis trade unwinds and shifting demand for long-duration Treasuries.

On tariff-exposed sectors like healthcare (XLV) and semiconductors (SMH), (SOXX), Shalett advised focusing on high-quality names with pricing power.

“You can’t count on anything as permanent,” she told CNBC, advocating for stock-picking in resilient industries, such as financials (XLF) and some healthcare (XLV) names that align with a disciplined value approach amid geopolitical crosscurrents.

Investors can watch the current bond action here.

data.title: March CPI data holds clues on weaker services inflation, Citi says
data.description:
While markets paid little attention to the March CPI data that came out on Thursday, Citi economist Veronica Clark saw valuable nuggets in the inflation reading.

Many observers expect new tariffs to fuel inflation, but Clark points out that the uncertain macro environment may lead to lower demand, which would fuel disinflation. 

In essence, goods prices are expected to rise with the application of higher tariff rates, but the prices of services may drop as demand weakens.

"While goods prices are likely to rise in the future, weakness in core services, excluding housing, in March likely reflects softer demand," Clark wrote in a note to clients.

The softer services pricing, excluding shelter, is "the most important and underappreciated element" of the report, she said. Excluding rents and owner-equivalent rent, core services prices declined 0.24% M/M, the largest M/M decline since May 2020.

"We expect services prices to remain soft with a weakening demand and labor market backdrop," she said. "In this context, soft March CPI is just as much a reflection of recent events as future tariff-induced price increases will be."

With the soft March CPI print, March core PCE (or personal consumption expenditures excluding food and energy) is tracking at +0.08% M/M and +2.6% Y/Y, she figures. The PCE data will be released on April 30 in the Personal Income and Outlays report.

Even with the shifting tariff narrative, Citi leaves its forecast for core PCE at 3.5% Q4/Q4. However, a number of considerations include: the increase in the effective tariff rate is about 18%; assuming tariffs reduce imports into the U.S. the effective rate would be lower; and the shifting "contours of tariffs could impact the timing of various inflation impacts." Thus, delays could mean smaller tariffs and reduced price increases.

data.title: Stocks get a boost from banks even as traders remain watchful of tariff developments
data.description:
Wall Street on Tuesday was holding on to gains, helped by a jump in financial stocks that were boosted by positive quarterly results from major banks. Market participants also kept a sharp eye on further developments surrounding tariffs. 

Approaching midday, the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500) was last +0.22% at 5,417.72 points, and was on track for a three-day win streak. The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +0.21% at 16,866.31 points, while the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was +0.09% at 40,560.82 points.

Of the 11 S&P sectors, six were in the green, led by a +1% rise in Financials.

The sector was boosted by a +4.2% gain in Bank of America (BAC) and a +2.8% advance in Citi (C). The number two and number four U.S. bank by assets, respectively, delivered double-digit Y/Y percentage growth in first quarter net income. 

BofA top boss Brian Moynihan noted "resilience" in consumers who have continued to spend and maintain healthy credit quality. Meanwhile, Citi CEO Jane Fraser reckoned that after all the trade wars, "the U.S. will still be the world’s leading economy, and the dollar will remain the reserve currency."

In other earnings-related moves, Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) was -0.6%. The Dow component upped its full-year operational sales guidance but maintained its adjusted profit per share growth outlook. 

Netflix (NFLX) was +5%, ahead of the streaming giant's quarterly report on Thursday.  

On the tariff front, Bloomberg News reported that the European Union (EU) and the U.S. had made little progress in trade negotiations, and that the EU expects the U.S. trade barriers to remain in place. 

Traders have had to digest several - sometimes confusing - updates on tariffs since Friday, but the overall picture has been one of positivity, helping boost sentiment on Monday. 

"Monday’s broad-based gains...saw the domestically-focused, mid-cap Russell 2000 lead the other majors, closing 1.1% higher on the day. This fresh positivity followed on from some encouraging, if confusing, tariff comments from the Trump administration after Friday’s market close," David Morrison, senior market analyst at Trade Nation, said.  

"President Trump refined his reciprocal tariffs once again, this time by exempting smartphones and other electronics. He also made it clear that the administration was looking at semiconductors and pharmaceuticals as the next sectors for specific levies. But this news was tempered after he also said he was considering temporary exemptions to tariffs on imported vehicles and parts. This would take some immediate pressure off Canada and Mexico whose auto supply chains are integral to U.S. car manufacturers," Morrison added.

Over in the fixed-income markets, U.S. Treasury yields stabilized after a bruising bond selloff in longer-term maturities last week. The benchmark 10-year yield (US10Y) was last down 2 basis points to 4.34%. The shorter-end more rate-sensitive 2-year yield (US2Y) was up 2 basis points to 3.85%.

For more, see how Treasury yields have done across the curve on the Seeking Alpha bond page.

data.title: Brazil oil regulator to grant rights in promising offshore area where Petrobras fights to drill
data.description:
Brazil’s oil regulator is set to grant exploration rights in the promising Equatorial Margin offshore region where state-controlled Petrobras (NYSE:PBR) has faced a years-long dispute with environmental authorities to start drilling, Bloomberg reported Tuesday.

A planned June 17 auction will include areas in five deepwater basins, including the Potiguar and the Foz do Amazonas, regulator ANP confirmed Monday; both areas are in the wider Equatorial Margin region, where the oil industry sees the best chances for Brazil to replenish oil reserves that otherwise will start declining in the 2030s.

Brazil will offer 172 oil blocks, including 47 at the Foz do Amazonas, where Petrobras (NYSE:PBR) is appealing to drill its first well after its exploration campaign was blocked in 2023; Brazil's Mines and Energy Ministry has said the basin could contain oil fields similar to those discovered in Suriname and Guyana, where an Exxon-led consortium has found billions of barrels.

"The lack of authorization for Petrobras to drill in the Foz do Amazonas basin brings uncertainty and certainly affects the attractiveness of the auction," A&M Infra consultancy director Rivaldo Moreira Neto told Bloomberg.

Meanwhile, the areas offered in the Potiguar basin are at risk of being left out of future rounds if they are not granted in June, because a joint statement from Brazil’s environment ministry and mines and energy ministry that allows the environmentally sensitive areas to be offered is set to expire the day after the oil auction, the report said.

ANP also will offer exploration acreage in southern Brazil's Pelotas basin, which is viewed as geologically similar to Namibia where European oil majors have made major discoveries.
